evals.prompt: Run the Ten Types diagnostic for our Composable DXP service portfolio. We deliver headless CMS implementations, integration architecture, design systems, and personalization stand-up. Where are we actually competing?
evals.expected_output: Should produce honest scoring across the ten types: (1) Profit Model (Type 1) — likely LOW; standard time-and-materials and fixed-fee, no profit-model innovation; opportunity for outcome-based or platform-share models; (2) Network (Type 2) — MEDIUM-HIGH; 15 ISV partner relationships is real Network innovation, but customer-perceptible? Probably under-leveraged; (3) Structure (Type 3) — MEDIUM; the practice itself is well-organized but probably not distinctive vs. peers; (4) Process (Type 4) — MEDIUM-HIGH; composable accelerator + delivery patterns are real Process innovation; (5) Product Performance (Type 5) — HIGH on technical execution, but features-of-services are generic; (6) Product System (Type 6) — MEDIUM; the integration of CMS + commerce + personalization is real but competitors do this; (7) Service (Type 7) — MEDIUM; standard managed-services post-launch; (8) Channel (Type 8) — LOW; Slalom-direct only, no innovative channel; (9) Brand (Type 9) — MEDIUM; Slalom brand is strong but Composable DXP brand is undifferentiated; (10) Customer Engagement (Type 10) — LOW; transactional engagement post-delivery, no community. Pattern: 'Process + Network depth, Brand and Customer Engagement weakness.' Recommendation: invest in Types 1, 9, 10 to compound differentiation — these are high-leverage, low-investment moves for a services practice.

evals.prompt: Design a 4-type combination for an AI-native customer-onboarding bet for credit unions.
evals.expected_output: Should brainstorm across all 10 types, then propose a defensible combination. Recommended combination: Type 5 (Product Performance — AI-driven 8-min onboarding) + Type 7 (Service — concierge fallback for edge cases, member-success follow-up) + Type 1 (Profit Model — per-onboarding fee with risk-share on completion-rate gains, not flat SaaS) + Type 10 (Customer Engagement — member onboarding becomes the entry point to a credit-union community/financial-wellness loop). Why this combination: (1) Type 5 alone gets copied within 12 months (vendors will replicate AI features); (2) Type 7 fallback addresses the trust assumption — credit unions are relationship-heavy, pure self-serve fails on emotional job; (3) Type 1 outcome-based pricing aligns vendor and credit-union incentives, creates switching cost for the credit union; (4) Type 10 turns a transactional onboarding into a relationship hook, defending the position. Hard to copy because: a competitor would need to replicate AI tech + concierge talent + outcome-pricing infrastructure + community platform — four-vector attack, not a feature copy. Note: this connects to Doblin's 'Make it stick' pattern (Types 6+7) and platform-economics intuition (Types 1+2).

evals.prompt: We've been getting copied for 3 years. Diagnose the type-pattern and recommend a Configuration-side response.
evals.expected_output: Should diagnose: getting-copied-quickly is a Type 5 (Product Performance) only signal. Each new feature you ship is matched in 6-12 months. The structural fix is Configuration-side investment (Types 1-4) which is much harder to copy. Recommendations: (1) Type 1 (Profit Model) — change how you make money. Subscription → outcome-share. Per-seat → consumption. Flat → tiered with usage-based add-ons. Profit-model innovation reframes the competitive comparison; (2) Type 2 (Network) — build ecosystem partnerships that are exclusive or near-exclusive. Two-sided network value compounds and can't be copied without rebuilding the network; (3) Type 3 (Structure) — rare but powerful. Talent network model, asset-light positioning, IP licensing structure. Examples: Costco's membership-financed model, Toyota's keiretsu; (4) Type 4 (Process) — productize the operating model. The Composable Accelerator pattern (Slalom-internal example) is Process innovation; replicating it requires institutional knowledge that won't transfer. Note: Configuration innovations are slow to design and hard to communicate but they create durable moats. Pair with marketing to translate Configuration innovation into customer-perceptible value.

evals.prompt: Brainstorm across all 10 types for a B2B contract intelligence platform.
evals.expected_output: Should produce 3-5 ideas per type, deliberately spanning Configuration and Experience. Examples: Type 1 — outcome-based pricing on $-saved through better contract terms; usage-based by document volume; Type 2 — ISV partnerships with CRM/ERP/legal stacks for embedded experiences; consortium of mid-market companies sharing anonymized contract benchmarks; Type 3 — distributed legal-AI experts model, asset-light; Type 4 — AI-native contract review process replacing manual paralegal flow; Type 5 — clause-extraction accuracy, redline workflow, audit trail; Type 6 — integrated suite (review + negotiate + manage + renew) vs. point-tool; Type 7 — concierge contract review for high-stakes deals; legal-counsel-on-demand; Type 8 — direct-to-procurement vs. through-legal channel; embedded in document signing tools; Type 9 — 'we make procurement professionals look brilliant' brand; Type 10 — community of procurement leaders sharing playbooks; certified user designation. Then propose a 4-5 type combination — likely Type 5 + Type 6 + Type 1 + Type 10 (capability + integration + pricing + community). Justify why these compound.

Should analyze Stripe across the ten types: (1) Type 5 (Product Performance) — HIGH: developer-friendly APIs, reliability, breadth of payment methods; (2) Type 4 (Process) — HIGH: continuous-deployment platform, fraud-detection ML pipeline, developer-onboarding experience; (3) Type 7 (Service) — HIGH: documentation, support, integration assistance — exceptional for B2B; (4) Type 2 (Network) — HIGH: bank partnerships, card-network integrations, regulatory licenses globally; (5) Type 1 (Profit Model) — MEDIUM-HIGH: transparent transparent pricing, multiple revenue streams (Connect, Atlas, Capital, Treasury); (6) Type 10 (Customer Engagement) — HIGH: developer community, education content, conference (Sessions); (7) Type 6 (Product System) — HIGH: integrated suite from payments to banking-as-a-service; (8) Type 9 (Brand) — HIGH: 'developer-first' brand identity; (9) Type 3 (Structure) — MEDIUM: distributed engineering culture; (10) Type 8 (Channel) — MEDIUM: API-first distribution. Pattern: Stripe scores high on 7-8 types — that's the source of its defensibility. Anyone trying to compete with payment APIs alone (Type 5) gets crushed by Stripe's network + process + service + community + system advantages. Lesson: durable B2B platforms compound across 5-8 types, not 1-2.

evals.prompt: Our innovation portfolio brainstorms produce only Product Performance ideas. How do I force the team into Configuration thinking?
evals.expected_output: Should diagnose the cognitive bias: product-trained teams default to Type 5 because that's what they know. Force-functions to break the pattern: (1) Type-quota brainstorm — require ≥3 ideas per type before any solution gets discussed; (2) Configuration-first session — ban Types 5, 6 for the first 60 minutes; force Types 1, 2, 3, 4 first; (3) Analog forcing — for each non-Product type, find a real-world example from another industry and ask 'how would [Costco / Stripe / Cirque] solve our problem?'; (4) Doblin examples deck — show the canonical three-type wins (Apple, Costco, Cirque, Method, Stripe) before brainstorming; (5) Profit-model lab — dedicated 90-min session on Type 1 only, with finance and pricing-strategist included; (6) Community-design lab — dedicated 90-min on Type 10 with cx-jtbd input. Coaching: most teams need 2-3 cycles before Configuration thinking becomes natural. Pair with leadership-coach to model the move (leader publicly proposes Configuration idea, normalizes the move). Note: the bias toward Type 5 is also a hiring signal — if every team member came from a product role, the team's typology will mirror their backgrounds. Diversify staffing for diversity of typology.

evals.prompt: Map our type-investment vs. our top 2 competitors. Where are we structurally vulnerable?
evals.expected_output: Should produce a 3-column heatmap (us, competitor A, competitor B) across all 10 types with relative scoring (0-5). Then identify structural vulnerabilities: (1) Types where we score lower than both competitors — direct gaps to close; (2) Types where competitors score similarly to us — table stakes, not differentiation; (3) Types where we score higher — defend and amplify; (4) Types where all three score low — opportunity space (whoever invests first wins). Recommend (a) close the gap on 1-2 highest-impact vulnerabilities (typically Types 1, 7, or 10), (b) amplify our 2-3 strongest types, (c) consider entering a low-investment open type for asymmetric advantage. Note that Configuration types (1-4) are most strategically important when assessing structural vulnerability — competitors who invest in Configuration become hard to dislodge; if you're behind on Configuration, the gap compounds. Hand off the gap-closing work to innovation-strategist (mandate decision) and innovation-portfolio-architect (capital allocation).

evals.prompt: We're considering acquiring a startup. What does the Ten Types lens tell us about whether to build, buy, or partner?
evals.expected_output: Should use the typology to surface acquisition rationale beyond financials. (1) Diagnostic question — what type does the startup represent that we're missing? Common answers: a Type 9 (Brand) we don't have credibility to build, a Type 2 (Network) we can't access, a Type 10 (Customer Engagement community) we can't seed, a Type 5 (Product Performance) capability that would take 18+ months to build. (2) If startup represents a missing type — strong rationale to acquire/partner; the buy-vs-build question becomes 'speed × asset value vs. integration cost'; (3) If startup represents same type as us — likely a feature acquisition, not a typology acquisition; weaker rationale; (4) Build path makes sense when — type is buildable in <12 months, talent is recruitable, brand carries; (5) Partner path makes sense when — type is durable but loose alliance gets the access without integration cost (esp. Type 2 Network plays); (6) Acquire path makes sense when — type is genuinely scarce, integration risk is manageable (Christensen separate-unit pattern), and timing matters strategically. Ten-Types framing surfaces whether the deal is about acquiring a *type* (high-leverage) or just acquiring a *feature* (low-leverage).
